candidate_problems.text: What principled criterion marks the boundary between mere control variables and genuinely representational states? The conjecture says intentionality appears when internal states are decouplable, action-guiding, and error-correctable, but many non-mental systems—thermostats, PID controllers, homeostatic circuits, predictive filters—also satisfy versions of these conditions. The unresolved tension is whether the conjecture overgenerates intentionality, or whether there is a sharper structural threshold (for example involving counterfactual richness, model-based recombination, latent-world inference, or multi-step planning) that distinguishes full aboutness from sophisticated causation.

How can semantic normativity be derived from survival-linked feedback without collapsing truth into mere usefulness? The conjecture ties aboutness to states whose continued viability depends on tracking the world, but this leaves open whether misrepresentation is genuinely world-relative or only instrumentally relative to fitness/control success. A systematically useful false model can guide successful action, while a true but unusable model may not. The open problem is to explain how truth, falsity, and error-content arise from control architecture in a way that is not reducible to evolutionary or pragmatic success alone.

Is survival pressure actually necessary for intentionality, or only one route to it? The conjecture privileges the edge where correlation is insufficient for survival, but this may be too biologically narrow. Artificial systems, mathematical reasoners, scientific instruments, and cultural-symbolic systems can exhibit world-tracking, corrigibility, and distal reference outside immediate survival contexts. The tension is whether intentionality fundamentally depends on organismic viability, or whether the deeper generator is a more general class of knowledge-creating systems that maintain corrigible internal surrogates under counterfactual testing.
